narrative: As a Black male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background and plans to attend law school full-time, your profile stood out in several ways, including a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.30—slightly above the typical 3.21 average—and an LSAT score of 35, which is close to the usual 36.70 mark. However, the university's admission model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on students' past academic and background details, calculated a 53.1% chance that you would fail the bar exam. This tipped the prediction just over the edge toward failure, leading to the rejection of your application. The model's decision hinged most heavily on your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, which placed you in the lower part of your class—well below the average decile of about 5.5—and this was the strongest factor pushing the prediction toward expecting a bar failure. Your undergraduate GPA played the next biggest role, but in a positive way, as its above-average strength pulled the prediction back toward passing the exam. Similarly, your LSAT score provided a helpful boost, nudging the outlook toward success compared to what lower scores might have done. Overall, while your GPA and LSAT were competitive with many applicants, the lower Year 3 performance outweighed them, signaling to the model a potential risk not seen as often in students who typically pass the bar on their first try. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Black man from a supportive background pursuing law full-time—it's just how these key academic signals stacked up against patterns from past students. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your later-year rankings, as even small improvements there could shift future predictions dramatically. You might also seek targeted LSAT or bar prep resources tailored for underrepresented students like yourself to build a stronger case next time. Admissions teams value persistence, so reapplying with enhanced credentials could open doors at this or other schools. Keep pushing—your story is far from over.
